description: Three dangerous issues hidden across a complex medication list. (1) Patient takes Coumadin at home — the hospital prescribed aspirin for post-surgical pain without recognizing Coumadin is warfarin, creating a major bleeding interaction. (2) Digoxin dose was silently doubled — a narrow therapeutic index drug where doubling the dose can cause fatal arrhythmia. (3) Patient's home beta-blocker metoprolol is completely missing from discharge — abrupt withdrawal causes rebound hypertension and can trigger a heart attack.
patient_context: 81-year-old female with atrial fibrillation, heart failure, and type 2 diabetes. Admitted for hip fracture repair. Three different specialists updated the medication list independently without cross-checking: cardiologist, orthopedic surgeon, and hospitalist.

planted_issues.description: Coumadin is the brand name for warfarin. Warfarin + aspirin is a major interaction — both inhibit clotting via different mechanisms, dramatically increasing hemorrhage risk post-surgery.
Digoxin dose doubled from 0.125mg to 0.25mg. Digoxin has an extremely narrow therapeutic index — doubling the dose can cause toxicity: bradycardia, heart block, and fatal arrhythmia.
Metoprolol (beta-blocker) is completely absent from the discharge list. Abrupt beta-blocker withdrawal causes rebound tachycardia, hypertension, and can precipitate a myocardial infarction.
